topic: Golang Wails vs Electron for a Kanban Board desktop application
executiveSummary: The panel converged fast on structure but not on the core empirical fact. Both specialists held their frameworks across the round, with two minor confidence shifts (native-go-engineer 0.62->0.55, web-frontend-pragmatist 0.70->~0.68), both triggered by the contrarian's stress-test (r1-msg-006). Four things are settled by argument: the Go backend is a red herring for the fidelity decision (r1-msg-004, r1-msg-005); fidelity is the gate and footprint the tiebreaker (r1-msg-004); Electron's footprint cost is real (r1-msg-001, r1-msg-005); and Electron's maintenance/TCO liability (Chromium upgrade treadmill + npm CVE churn) is real and conceded (r1-msg-006, r1-msg-008). What stays open is the load-bearing crux: whether one bundled Chromium is essential insurance against cross-webview drag failure or just unnecessary weight (r1-msg-002 vs r1-msg-007 vs r1-msg-008). This is unresolved by design — it can only be settled by a fleet-matrix PoC that does not yet exist, and the contrarian's deepest point (r1-msg-006) is that even a green PoC is n-of-1 and may not predict fleet behavior. The defensible recommendation is therefore conditional, gated on a matrixed PoC, with a quantified-TCO comparison as the second open question.

insights.title: Drag fidelity, not footprint, is the decision gate
insights.description: Both specialists agreed the decision hinges on whether dnd-kit drag/animation behaves identically across WebKit (macOS), WebView2 (Windows) and WebKitGTK (Linux). Footprint is real but only breaks ties. The pragmatist made the fidelity risk concrete by naming five WebKitGTK failure modes (pointer-capture loss, translate3d subpixel jitter, scroll-during-drag, backdrop-filter, sticky headers), which the go-engineer did not refute and instead promoted into the PoC matrix.
insights.confidenceReason: Resolved by argument, not majority: the gate/tiebreaker framing (r1-msg-004) was explicitly accepted by the opposing side (r1-msg-005, r1-msg-008) and the failure modes are concrete and specific.

supportingEvidence.summary: Proposed the rule: fidelity is the gate, footprint the tiebreaker
Hardened the gate into a fleet matrix covering those failure modes

insights.title: The Go backend is a red herring for the fidelity decision
insights.description: Both specialists agreed that since a Kanban board is not CPU-bound, the Go backend's performance advantage is not load-bearing for the choice (r1-msg-004, r1-msg-005). Caveat: the contrarian (r1-msg-006) showed this over-reached — Go remains relevant to MAINTENANCE/TCO even if irrelevant to fidelity, reclaimed in r1-msg-007.
insights.confidenceReason: Resolved by argument and conceded by the side it favors (r1-msg-004); pre-identified by the moderator (r1-msg-003); the contrarian's correction sharpened rather than overturned it.

supportingEvidence.summary: Moderator flagged Go backend as possible red herring since not CPU-bound
Go-engineer conceded Go backend is closer to a red herring
Contrarian narrowed it: red herring for fidelity, but NOT for maintenance/TCO

insights.title: One bundled Chromium is variance-reduction insurance, not variance-elimination
insights.description: The pragmatist's strongest move (r1-msg-008) reframed the contrarian's 'PoC is n-of-1' attack as PRO-Electron: bundling one Chromium collapses the QA matrix from N webviews to 1 — insurance against the cases a PoC cannot sample. Self-limited: Chromium still has ANGLE/GL and Wayland fractional-scaling bugs, so this reduces variance rather than eliminating it. Single most load-bearing pro-Electron argument; NOT refuted.
insights.confidenceReason: Strong by argument and honestly bounded, but its decisiveness depends on the unresolved empirical crux (how much cross-webview variance actually exists); logically sound but its weight is unmeasured.

supportingEvidence.summary: Reframed n-of-1 weakness as insurance: one Chromium reduces QA variance N->1, with 'reduction not elimination' caveat
Original thesis: one bundled Chromium = identical drag fidelity across 3 OSes

dissentingViews.summary: Counters that a fleet matrix PoC can verify fidelity directly, making bundling unnecessary weight rather than insurance

insights.title: Electron's TCO liability is real and conceded, but scoped
insights.description: The contrarian's TCO argument (r1-msg-006) — Chromium upgrade treadmill + npm CVE churn — was owned by the pragmatist (r1-msg-008) but scoped to 'scheduled chore + Renovate triage'. The go-engineer reclaimed a bounded counter-asset for Wails: no npm CVE tree, no self-rebundled Chromium, no Electron major-version treadmill, with the caveat that Wails inherits OS-webview variance support cost and a thinner frontend ecosystem.
insights.confidenceReason: Resolved by argument (conceded by Electron's advocate), but magnitude is unquantified — both sides asserted scope ('chore' vs 'treadmill') without numbers.

supportingEvidence.summary: Argued dominant long-run cost is maintenance/TCO, unmeasured by any PoC
Owned TCO as conceded liability, scoped to scheduled chore + Renovate
Reclaimed bounded Go maintenance advantage with honest caveats

insights.title: Tauri is a footprint hedge, not a fidelity hedge
insights.description: The contrarian introduced Tauri as a cheap third outcome (r1-msg-006). The go-engineer adopted it pragmatically (r1-msg-007): same dnd-kit frontend on both Wails and Tauri, so the host is a swap not a rewrite. The pragmatist's rebuttal (r1-msg-008) is decisive and un-refuted on the narrow point — Tauri uses the SAME system WebKitGTK on Linux, inheriting the identical fidelity risk; it hedges footprint (and adds a Rust tax) but does nothing for the gate.
insights.confidenceReason: Resolved by argument: Tauri sharing the system webview is a verifiable architectural fact, not contested; the go-engineer's adoption was about migration cost, not fidelity.

supportingEvidence.summary: Proposed Tauri as a cheap 3rd-outcome hedge
Tauri inherits WebKitGTK fidelity risk; footprint hedge only + Rust tax
Adopted Tauri so a host switch is a swap not a rewrite

agreements: Go backend is a red herring for the FIDELITY decision (a Kanban board is not CPU-bound) (r1-msg-004, r1-msg-005), though it remains relevant to maintenance/TCO (r1-msg-006, r1-msg-007).
Drag/animation fidelity is the gate; footprint is the tiebreaker (r1-msg-004, accepted r1-msg-005/r1-msg-008).
Electron's footprint cost is real and not disputed (r1-msg-001, r1-msg-005).
Electron's maintenance/TCO liability (Chromium treadmill + npm CVE churn) is real and conceded, even if its magnitude is unquantified (r1-msg-006, r1-msg-008).
The cross-webview drag-identity claim is currently UNTESTED and must be demonstrated, not assumed (r1-msg-004).

minorityReport.position: A green PoC does not guarantee green production: a 1-week test is n-of-1, and even a fleet matrix cannot sample real-world GPU drivers, fractional DPI, IME pointercancel theft, and 18-month-stale distro WebKitGTK across the actual user fleet.
minorityReport.reason: The go-engineer's response (r1-msg-007) hardened the PoC into a fleet matrix but did not prove a matrix is fleet-VALID — it expanded sampling without closing the gap between sample and population.
minorityReport.note: The un-refuted dissent that keeps the PoC-gated consensus epistemically humble. It is the strongest single argument FOR Electron's variance-collapse insurance (r1-msg-008), and why the core crux is unresolved by design.

minorityReport.position: The dominant long-run cost is maintenance/TCO, which no PoC measures; the fidelity-gate framing risks optimizing the cheaply-measurable thing while leaving the expensive thing unquantified.
minorityReport.reason: Both sides accepted TCO matters (r1-msg-007, r1-msg-008) but neither produced numbers; the liability was scoped rhetorically, not measured.
minorityReport.note: Partially absorbed into consensus but the quantification gap remains — see open question 2.

unresolvedDebates.topic: Is one bundled Chromium load-bearing insurance or unnecessary weight?
positions: Pragmatist: bundling collapses the QA variance space N->1; insurance against exactly the cases a PoC cannot sample (r1-msg-008).
Go-engineer: a fleet-matrix PoC can verify fidelity directly, making the 120-180MB Chromium pure overhead once fidelity is demonstrated (r1-msg-007).
unresolvedDebates.whyUnresolved: An empirical question with no data: the deciding evidence (does cross-webview drag actually diverge, and by how much) requires a fleet-matrix PoC that does not exist. Each side's framing is internally consistent; only measurement can break the tie.

unresolvedDebates.topic: Can a (matrixed) PoC ever be fleet-valid?
positions: Go-engineer: a sufficiently broad matrix (old/new GPU, two distro WebKitGTK incl. stale LTS, fractional DPI, IME, a11y) is decision-grade (r1-msg-007).
Contrarian: any finite matrix is still a sample; green-on-matrix does not entail green-on-fleet because the failure space is combinatorially larger than any harness (r1-msg-006).
unresolvedDebates.whyUnresolved: A methodological/epistemic disagreement about inference from sample to population. Running the PoC only generates one more sample — the contrarian's whole point. It can only be bounded by choosing an acceptable residual-risk threshold, which the panel never set.

unresolvedDebates.topic: Is Tauri a real third option or only a footprint hedge?
positions: Go-engineer: worth adopting because it makes the host a swappable choice over a shared dnd-kit frontend (r1-msg-007).
Pragmatist: it shares system WebKitGTK so carries the identical fidelity risk; only a footprint hedge plus a Rust tax (r1-msg-008).
unresolvedDebates.whyUnresolved: Resolved on the narrow technical fact (Tauri does not solve fidelity — r1-msg-008 un-refuted), but unresolved on VALUE: whether footprint-hedge-plus-portability is worth the Rust tax depends on team-skill and TCO numbers, neither quantified.

journey: Wails on footprint + Go backend, conf 0.62 -> Wails on a fleet-matrix-gated fidelity bet + bounded Go-maintenance argument + Tauri portability, conf 0.55, never flipped
keyTurningPoints: r1-msg-004: conceded cross-webview drag identity is untested and Go backend is closer to a red herring; conf 0.62->0.55; proposed PoC and the gate/tiebreaker rule
r1-msg-007: hardened PoC into a fleet matrix, reclaimed a bounded Go MAINTENANCE argument, adopted Tauri as a swap-not-rewrite hedge

journey: Electron on one-Chromium drag identity + React ecosystem, conf 0.70 -> Electron on variance-REDUCTION insurance, owning TCO as a scoped liability, rejecting Tauri as a fidelity hedge, conf ~0.68, never flipped
keyTurningPoints: r1-msg-005: conceded footprint is real and Go backend a red herring; named 5 concrete WebKitGTK drag-failure modes
r1-msg-008: turned the contrarian's n-of-1 attack into the thesis (bundling = insurance), owned TCO as conceded-but-scoped, refuted Tauri as fidelity hedge; conf 0.70->~0.68

openQuestions.question: Does dnd-kit drag/animation actually diverge across WebKit / WebView2 / WebKitGTK on the target fleet, and at what severity?
openQuestions.whyOpen: The entire decision pivots on this and there is zero data; the claim was conceded UNTESTED in r1-msg-004 and never tested.
openQuestions.suggestedApproach: Build the fleet-matrix PoC: a single dnd-kit Kanban exercising all 5 named failure modes, run across old+new GPU on WebView2; two distro WebKitGTK incl. ~18mo-stale LTS; fractional DPI 125%/150% pointer-offset; IME/CJK pointercancel; accessibility pass. Define a PASS threshold and acceptable residual-risk bound BEFORE running.

openQuestions.question: What is the quantified 3-year TCO of Electron's Chromium-upgrade-treadmill + npm CVE triage versus Wails/Tauri's OS-webview-variance support cost + thinner-ecosystem tax?
openQuestions.whyOpen: Both sides conceded TCO matters and scoped it rhetorically but produced no numbers; the contrarian's point (r1-msg-006) that the expensive thing went unmeasured stands.
openQuestions.suggestedApproach: Estimate engineer-days/year for (a) Electron Chromium bumps + builder/updater upkeep + CVE triage and (b) Wails/Tauri per-OS webview regressions + missing-component reimplementation; multiply by the team's actual Go/Rust-vs-React skill mix.

recommendations.action: Default to Electron when drag/animation fidelity is mission-critical, the team is React-deep with thin Go/Rust skills, the fleet is broad/uncontrolled (consumer Linux distros, varied GPUs/DPI), and footprint is not a hard constraint. Bundled Chromium's variance-reduction (r1-msg-008) is the rational hedge when you cannot afford the PoC risk or control the deployment environment.
recommendations.risk: Pays a permanent 120-180MB footprint and accepts the conceded TCO treadmill (r1-msg-006, r1-msg-008) — real costs for insurance whose necessity is unproven.
recommendations.prerequisite: Acceptance that footprint and Chromium-cadence maintenance are acceptable ongoing costs; budget for Renovate/CVE triage and scheduled Electron major upgrades.

recommendations.action: Choose Wails when a fleet-matrix PoC comes back GREEN on all named failure modes, footprint matters (kiosk/embedded/resource-constrained), and the team has real Go strength. Once fidelity is demonstrated, the 8-15MB binary plus the bounded Go-maintenance advantage (r1-msg-007) wins on the tiebreaker.
recommendations.risk: If the PoC is green but a matrix is not fleet-valid (r1-msg-006, un-refuted), production drag bugs surface post-ship across uncontrolled distros/GPUs.
recommendations.prerequisite: Run the fleet-matrix PoC AND pre-commit to a residual-risk threshold; do not ship on a green n-of-1.

recommendations.action: Consider Tauri only as a footprint/portability hedge, NOT a fidelity solution: it shares system WebKitGTK and inherits the identical Linux drag risk (r1-msg-008). Justified when you want the small footprint and a swappable host over a shared dnd-kit frontend (r1-msg-007) and can absorb the Rust tax.
recommendations.risk: Spending the Rust tax to hedge footprint while the actual gate (Linux fidelity) is unchanged versus Wails.
recommendations.prerequisite: The same fleet-matrix PoC must pass on Tauri's Linux WebKitGTK.

recommendations.action: Gate the whole decision on the fleet-matrix PoC plus the TCO estimate before committing a framework; treat the green/red PoC outcome and the TCO delta as the two inputs that select among the three rules above.
recommendations.risk: Low — process, not a framework bet; the only cost is the ~1-week PoC.
recommendations.prerequisite: Allocate the ~1-week PoC budget and define PASS/residual-risk criteria up front.

metaObservations: The discussion converged structurally with unusual speed — by r1-msg-005 both specialists had conceded each other's strongest non-core points (footprint real, Go-backend red herring), the mark of high-quality steel-manning rather than entrenchment. Both position shifts were minor and both triggered by the SAME message (r1-msg-006): the contrarian did the round's real work, not by flipping anyone but by forcing both sides onto firmer ground. The deepest signal is that the unresolved crux is EMPIRICAL, not architectural — the panel correctly identified that no further argument can settle whether cross-webview drag diverges, because that is a measurement. The honest output is a decision RULE plus the experiment that selects within it. Caution: the fast convergence on 'fidelity is the gate' may itself be a shared blind spot — only the contrarian pushed that TCO may be the larger long-run cost; with a 1-round lightweight budget that dissent is recorded but under-explored, which is why both the PoC and the TCO estimate are kept as live open questions.
